metadata.title: Why 1 + 1/4 + 1/9 + … Equals π²/6
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = sum / π²/6 / geometric proof / lighthouse; CRIMSON = integers / squares / why pi appears at all
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no Euler sine-series proof, no inscribed angle theorem proof, no Riemann zeta generalization. Wonder register.

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: The sum 1 plus 1 over 4 plus 1 over 9 plus 1 over 16 — sum of reciprocals of all perfect squares — stumped mathematicians for 90 years. Euler found the answer: π squared over 6. What does pi have to do with perfect squares?
The sum is called the Basel problem. It converges. First four terms: 1.424. First 1000 terms: 1.6440. The limit is 1.6449 — which equals π squared over 6.

production_viz.label: Partial sum bar chart converging: 4 terms=1.424, 1000 terms=1.6440, limit π²/6=1.6449; TEAL bars converging to π²/6 line

beats.narration_text: Why does summing reciprocals of squares of integers give a number involving π? Where does π sneak in — and what geometric structure makes it appear?

card.copy: 1 + 1/4 + 1/9 + 1/16 + … = π²/6. Why does π appear in a sum about integers?

beats.narration_text: One elegant approach: think of lighthouses on a number line at positions 1, 2, 3, and so on in both directions. Each lighthouse has brightness proportional to 1 over distance squared — the inverse square law. Total brightness from all lighthouses equals the Basel sum.

production_viz.label: Number line with lighthouse icons at 1, 2, 3, 4; brightness bars shrinking as 1/n²; TEAL for total brightness = Basel sum

beats.narration_text: Draw a circle of radius 1 passing through the observer and through the lighthouse at position 1. By the inscribed angle theorem, angles from the observer to the two nearest lighthouses on this circle relate to the circle's geometry.

production_viz.label: Circle through observer and lighthouse at position 1; inscribed angle theorem shown; TEAL for circle geometry

beats.narration_text: Repeated circle-doubling: each step rearranges the lighthouses onto a new circle, preserving total brightness by the inscribed angle theorem. After many doublings, the circles become very large — essentially flat.

production_viz.label: Circle doubling sequence: small circle → larger circle → very large near-flat circle; lighthouses rearranging; TEAL for preserved brightness

beats.narration_text: In the flat limit, the total brightness from all lighthouses on both sides of a very large circle corresponds to the circumference of the original circle — which is 2π. Working back through the geometry gives the Basel sum equals π squared over 6.

production_viz.label: Flat limit: very large circle flattening to number line; 2π circumference appears; TEAL arrow from 2π back to π²/6 result

beats.narration_text: Euler's original proof was different — he factored the sine function as an infinite product of its roots and compared coefficients. Both proofs arrive at the same answer. The geometric lighthouse proof makes π's appearance visual.

production_viz.label: Two paths to π²/6: Euler's sine factoring (CRIMSON historical path) and lighthouse circle-doubling (TEAL geometric path); same answer

beats.narration_text: The Basel sum is the Riemann zeta function evaluated at 2: ζ(2) equals π squared over 6. This connection between the zeta function and π runs deep through number theory and complex analysis.

production_viz.label: ζ(2) = π²/6 equation displayed; Riemann zeta function label; TEAL for deep connection to number theory

beats.narration_text: Sum of 1 over n squared equals π squared over 6. Lighthouse picture: total brightness from all integers equals the Basel sum. Circle-doubling preserves brightness. Flat limit: circumference 2π appears. Euler 1734.

card.copy: Σ(1/n²) = π²/6. Lighthouses at integers: brightness 1/r². Circle-doubling preserves total. Flat limit → 2π → π²/6. Euler 1734. Basel sum = ζ(2).

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
